steps.title: Identify the grammatical relationship and the punctuation rule at stake
steps.content: The blank fills in the subject of the verb compares. The structure is: “pioneering [blank] compares the experiences…” The blank must contain the noun playwright and the proper name Wakako Yamauchi. “Wakako Yamauchi” is an appositive that identifies which playwright is meant. Because removing the name would leave the sentence ambiguous (“pioneering playwright” could refer to anyone), the appositive is restrictive and takes no commas. Additionally, no comma can appear between the subject and its verb compares.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘playwright, Wakako Yamauchi,’: Commas on both sides treat the name as non-restrictive (removable), which is incorrect here, and the trailing comma also separates subject from verb. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘playwright, Wakako Yamauchi’: A comma after “playwright” incorrectly implies the name is non-restrictive, and the missing closing comma creates an asymmetric, ungrammatical structure. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘playwright Wakako Yamauchi,’: No comma before the name (correct for a restrictive appositive), but the trailing comma incorrectly separates the subject from the verb compares. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘playwright Wakako Yamauchi’: No commas anywhere — the restrictive appositive is unpunctuated and no comma interrupts the subject-verb link. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
